text: The surgeon uses five ports for laparoscopic fundoplication, including a 10mm port for optics and 5mm ports for liver retraction and working instruments. A Natanson liver retractor is inserted, revealing adhesions on the left lobe of the liver. Additional ports are inserted for the surgeon's left and right hands and the assistant.

The surgeon performs gasolysis using harmonic scissors with ultrasound energy to retract the liver for better exposure of the operation field. The stomach and cardia are removed from the chest into the abdominal cavity, and the assistant fixes the fundus in place. The pars flaccida is identified and divided in a vascular zone using harmonic scissors. The hepatic branch of the vagus nerve is visible, and the surgeon uses the right crux as a landmark to proceed.

The surgeon identifies the right crus and proceeds along the anterior semicircle towards the left crus. The cleavage plane between the right crus and esophagus is located, and the surgeon navigates through this plane from right to left.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to divide the phrenoesophageal membrane over the esophagus, revealing the cleavage plane between the esophagus and the right and left crura. The posterior mediastinum becomes visible, and care is taken to avoid the posterior vagus nerve. The fundus of the stomach is partially mobilized for fundoplication, with the short gastric vessels identified and coagulated using ultrasound energy.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the fundus of the stomach and proceeds to mobilize the posterior wall of the stomach and the left crux. Attention is given to the posterior gastric artery, which may be present in this area. The remnants of the frontal fundus ligament are divided, occasionally using a hook instrument.

The surgeon divides the tissue fixing the stomach to the chest, exposing the clear left pleura. They navigate the cleavage plane between the left cruse and esophagus, entering the retroesophageal space. The fundus is visible through this window. Using an esophageal retractor, the remnants of the retroesophageal membrane, known as the membrane of Lyman-Bortelli, are divided. Care is taken to avoid the posterior vagus nerve.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the esophagus, revealing the anterior vagus nerve. Using blunt dissection, the cleavage plane between the esophagus and left crux is accessed, leading to the posterior mediastinum. The remnants of the front esophageal ligament and phrenogastric ligament are divided. The fundus is visible through the window, and the surgeon performs the shoe shine maneuver, ensuring tension-free fundoplication. Cautery is used carefully near the esophagus, with small portions exposed. The left mobilization of the esophagus is completed, extending the abdominal part of the esophagus by two to three centimeters. Posterior crurography is initiated using Etibond 2-0 sutures.

The surgeon situates the left crura, taking care to avoid damaging the aorta, and situates the right cruse while avoiding the inferior vena cava. An octagonal stitch is used for posterior crurography, with overlapping to bring the grooves closer together. The knots are tied loosely on the diaphragm to prevent ischemia and atrophy of the grooves.

The surgeon observes a decrease in the esophageal orifice size and performs anterior crurography. Using the Nissen rosette short floppy technique, the surgeon places two or three interrupted sutures for fundoplication, utilizing Etibond II sutures.

The surgeon applies a second interrupted suture between the stomach wall and the remains of the frontal esophageal ligament to prevent slippage syndrome. The fundoplication appears floppy, short, and without tension. The final view shows the posterior and anterior crurography, an elongated abdominal part of the esophagus, and a short, floppy Nissen Rosetti fundoplication.
